critic_raw: The design uses 'patient age (if available)' as metadata. Age availability may be inconsistent across institutions — if the held-out test institution has different age data availability than training institutions, the feature engineering would differ. Also, the design uses 'all features are computed from information available at the time of diagnosis' — but patient age is available at diagnosis time, so its inclusion is correct. However, if age is missing for a significant subset of cases in one institution, the imputation strategy (fit on training) may not transfer well.
defender_raw: Round 1: The 'if available' qualifier acknowledges that age may not be available across all institutions. If the held-out institution lacks age data, the model receives a missing-value imputation for age — the training-set imputation strategy handles this. Both multimodal and image-only baseline models receive the same metadata inputs (or lack thereof), preserving comparison fairness. Missing age would affect both models equally. The per-site analysis would reveal if age-missing cases perform differently, as an honest characterization of the data quality impact.

rounds.critic_followup: If age is available in training institutions but unavailable in the held-out test institution, the model was trained with age as a feature but tested without it. This is a feature distribution shift — the model learned to use age as a signal, but that signal is now missing. This could systematically degrade test performance beyond what a model without age would achieve.
rounds.defender_followup: This is a valid deployment concern. However, the model trained with age uses it as one feature among many (image embedding, text embedding, stain protocol). Models trained with features that are sometimes missing can use the missing indicator as signal — the imputed value (training-set mean age) would be fed to both multimodal and image-only models, applying the same substitution. If age is an important predictor, its absence would reduce performance for both models equally. The per-site macro-F1 analysis would reveal if the held-out institution (without age) performs worse — this is an honest finding about the deployment constraint.

adjudication_raw: One concern: age feature available in training but potentially missing in held-out institution. Addressed: imputation applied equally to both models; per-site analysis would reveal age-absence impact. Verdict: defense_wins.
